summary: Stellar Scarlet>Fire, strong second‑set jams, solid overall playing
blurb: The show shines with a fiery Scarlet Begonias → Fire On The Mountain transition and a high‑energy Iko Iko after an expansive drums segment. Jerry’s vocals and guitar work are highlighted throughout, while the second set maintains tight flow to a sweet Sugar Magnolia close.
review: April 11, 1978 at Atlanta’s Fox Theater captures the Dead in fine form. The first set rolls along with confident renditions of classics like Bertha and Friend Of The Devil, but it’s the second‑set that truly lifts the night – a seamless Looks Like Rain into Scarlet Begonias segue sets up an unforgettable Scarlet>Fire pairing, followed by Samson & Delilah and a soaring Terrapin Station. A 20‑minute drums stretch featuring Mickey’s sparkling steel‑pan textures leads straight into a raucous Iko Iko before the band wraps the set with a joyous Sugar Magnolia. Jerry’s vocal diction is crisp and his melodic lead on Fire shines, while Bob injects humor and strong backing vocals (Donna’s contributions are noted). Keith’s keyboards sit a bit back compared to earlier shows, but the overall balance of instruments keeps the groove tight. The crowd‑pleasing encore, Johnny B. Goode, adds a fitting rock‑n‑roll finish.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three of the four recordings describe the show as mixed but consistently praise the Scarlet>Fire sequence, Iko Iko, and strong second‑set flow while noting only isolated weak spots (Brown‑Eyed Women, Good Lovin', Sunrise). No major performance flaws are reported across sources, yielding a modest positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crisp soundboard capture with balanced mix, highlighting the standout Looks Like Rain → Scarlet Begonias and a smoking Scarlet > Fire, plus solid second‑set flow.

key_highlights: Scarlet Begonias → Fire On The Mountain transition shines with tight improvisation
20‑minute drums segment featuring Mickey’s steel‑pan textures
Iko Iko delivers high‑energy momentum after the drums

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s vocal diction is crisp and his melodic guitar work on the Scarlet → Fire segment is highlighted as a standout, with bluesy picking and an inventive solo that keep the energy high.
band_performance.Bob: Weir adds humor and personality to the set, while his backing vocals (and Donna’s contributions) are praised; occasional guitar short‑out noted but doesn’t detract from overall vibe.
band_performance.Keith: Keyboard work is described as less distinctive and somewhat faded compared to earlier shows, sitting further back in the mix.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums segment is a major highlight, with Mickey’s steel‑pan textures adding sparkle to an expansive 20‑minute jam.
